content.problemStatement:
There are NNN sweets in the store. The cost of the ithi^{th}ith sweet is AiA_iAi​ rupees. Chef is a regular customer, so **after** buying the ithi^{th}ith sweet, he gets a **cashback** of BiB_iBi​ rupees.

Chef has RRR rupees. He is fond of all the sweets, so he wants you to calculate the maximum number of sweets he can buy. Note that he can buy the same type of sweet multiple times, as long as he has the money to do so.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain TTT, the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of three lines of input.
The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and RRR — the number of sweets in the shop and the amount of money Chef has.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.
The third line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers B1,B2,…,BNB_1, B_2, \ldots, B_NB1​,B2​,…,BN​.


Output Format
For each query, print on a new line the maximum number of sweets Chef can buy.
constraints: 1 ≤ N ≤ 2·10^5 (sum of N over all test cases ≤ 2·10^5)
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of three lines:
- The first line contains two integers N and R — the number of different sweets and Chef's initial amount of money.
- The second line contains N integers A1, A2, …, AN — the price of each sweet.
- The third line contains N integers B1, B2, …, BN — the cashback received after buying the corresponding sweet.
It is guaranteed that 1 ≤ Bi < Ai for every i.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the maximum number of sweets Chef can buy.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: Chef can buy sweet 1 (cost 2, cashback 1) twice, ending with 0 money.\nTest case 2: Only sweet 2 (cost 5, cashback 4) can be bought once.\nTest case 3: Minimum net cost is 2 (sweet 3). Starting with sweet 1 (cost 4, cashback 1) gives 1 + (10-4)/2 = 4 sweets, but starting with sweet 3 (cost 5, cashback 4) gives 1 + (10-5)/2 = 3 sweets. The best sequence yields 7 sweets.

solution.approach:
For each sweet i the net amount of money spent after the transaction is Di = Ai - Bi (Di ≥ 1). After buying any sweet, Chef's money decreases by Di, but to make a purchase he must have at least Ai rupees before the transaction. The optimal strategy is:
1. Compute d = min(Di) – the cheapest net cost among all sweets.
2. For every sweet i that Chef can afford initially (Ai ≤ R), assume he buys this sweet first and then repeats the sweet with net cost d as many times as possible. The number of sweets bought in this scenario is 1 + floor((R - Ai) / d).
3. The answer is the maximum value obtained in step 2. If no sweet can be afforded initially, the answer is 0.
The algorithm runs in O(N) per test case and uses O(1) extra memory.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total N) over all test cases.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = list(map(int, sys.stdin.read().split()))
    it = iter(data)
    T = next(it)
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = next(it); R = next(it)
        A = [next(it) for _ in range(N)]
        B = [next(it) for _ in range(N)]
        d = min(a-b for a,b in zip(A,B))
        ans = 0
        for a in A:
            if a <= R:
                cnt = 1 + (R - a)//d
                if cnt > ans:
                    ans = cnt
        out_lines.append(str(ans))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
